L1.area: Virtual machine runtime and bytecode execution layer

L2.description: An integer signedness error exists in a JavaScript engine's bytecode interpreter where a jump offset table entry is read as an unsigned integer instead of a signed integer. When a switch instruction requires a backward branch, the unsigned interpretation causes the offset to be treated as a large positive value instead of a negative one, leading to incorrect memory access. Attackers can exploit this via crafted JavaScript to cause denial of service or potentially achieve remote code execution.

L3.area: Bytecode interpreter's switch instruction dispatch and jump table offset handling
L3.description: An integer signedness error exists in the bytecode interpreter's switch instruction implementation where a jump table offset is read through an unsigned integer pointer instead of a signed integer pointer. When a switch case requires a backward branch, the unsigned read misinterprets the negative offset as a large positive value, leading to out-of-bounds memory access. This is exploitable via crafted JavaScript when the application permits evaluation of untrusted scripts.

ground_truth.reason: The SwitchImm bytecode instruction reads jump table offsets through an unsigned integer pointer instead of a signed one, causing backward branch offsets to be misinterpreted as large positive values, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.
